title: Whether neighbors owe each other names
summary: Argue whether knowing and using your neighbours' names constitutes a genuine social obligation or whether anonymity in dense urban settings is a legitimate choice.
topic: Whether neighbors owe each other names
prompt: Write an argumentative essay (350–500 words) on whether knowing your neighbours' names is a genuine social obligation or whether anonymity between neighbours is a legitimate choice in modern urban life. Refer to at least one concrete example (e.g. the high-density residential blocks of Hong Kong or Tokyo, or the 'Nextdoor' neighbourhood app used in the US and UK). Use a what-cleft sentence, a concession, and at least one passive construction. Take a clear position.
hintStarters: What a name shared between neighbours creates is not intimacy but something more modest and more…
Granted, in the high-density residential blocks of Hong Kong or Tokyo, the expectation that…
When someone needs to knock on a door during a crisis, the absence of a name…

hintVocab.meaningVi: bị coi là xâm phạm

sampleText:
What a name shared between neighbours creates is not intimacy but something more modest and more necessary: the minimum social infrastructure of a shared building. To know that the woman in apartment 4B is called Mrs Okafor is not to know her life or to claim a friendship she has not offered. It is simply to establish that she is a person who exists in your proximity — one whose absence during a crisis might be noticed, whose distress might be responded to, and who might do the same for you.

The case for anonymity has genuine force. In the high-density residential blocks of Hong Kong and Tokyo, where hundreds of households share walls and lifts and stairwells, the expectation of social connection can feel like an imposition. Urban life has historically functioned as a bargain: the city offers anonymity in exchange for proximity, and many people move to cities precisely because they do not want the obligations of a village. To require a name is, in that reading, to threaten the agreement.

Granted, this argument cannot be dismissed. Privacy is a legitimate interest, and the discomfort of being approached by neighbours whose values, habits, or backgrounds differ from your own is real. A name freely exchanged can be used as a vehicle for prejudice as much as for solidarity.

Even so, the refusal to acknowledge a neighbour by name is not a neutral act — it is a small, deliberate withdrawal from the architecture of mutual recognition that makes communal living survivable. When the Hong Kong apartment fires of the 2010s were studied for survivor behaviour, researchers found that prior knowledge of neighbours' names correlated with faster responses to knocks at the door and higher rates of evacuation assistance. The name is not a social luxury; it is a minimal mechanism of the low-level trust on which emergency response depends.

What is lost when neighbours refuse each other's names is not the warmth of community — which nobody is owed — but the thin layer of acknowledgement that separates a neighbour from a stranger. That layer has no weight until the moment it is needed. At that point, the name is not a courtesy; it is the difference between a door that opens and one that stays shut.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: What-cleft to define the minimal claim precisely
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What a name shared between neighbours creates is not intimacy but something more modest and more necessary' — what-cleft không chỉ nhấn mạnh điều được tạo ra ('something modest and necessary') mà cũng sử dụng 'not … but' để kiểm soát kỳ vọng của độc giả: tác giả chủ động loại bỏ đòi hỏi lớn hơn (intimacy) để làm cho đòi hỏi thực sự của mình (minimal infrastructure) có vẻ hợp lý hơn.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What a name shared between neighbours creates is not intimacy but something more modest and more necessary' uses the what-cleft to specify precisely what is being claimed. The 'not … but' also manages the reader's expectations — by explicitly denying the large claim (intimacy), the writer makes the real, modest claim harder to reject.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Passive in reported research to add authority without hedging
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'prior knowledge of neighbours' names correlated with faster responses to knocks at the door and higher rates of evacuation assistance' — mặc dù không hoàn toàn bị động, cấu trúc này bỏ qua chủ thể (những nhà nghiên cứu) và tập trung vào kết quả, tạo ra giọng điệu khoa học, khách quan. So sánh với 'researchers found that prior knowledge correlated' — phiên bản bị động nhấn mạnh phát hiện, không phải người tìm ra.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: Constructing the reported finding as 'prior knowledge of neighbours' names correlated with faster responses' (omitting the subject) focuses the reader on the finding rather than the finder, lending the claim authority. This contrasts with 'researchers found that…' — the agentless version feels more like an established fact.

sampleTranslationVi:
Điều mà một cái tên được chia sẻ giữa những người hàng xóm tạo ra không phải là sự thân mật mà là điều gì đó khiêm tốn hơn và cần thiết hơn: cơ sở hạ tầng xã hội tối thiểu của một tòa nhà chung. Biết rằng người phụ nữ ở căn hộ 4B tên là bà Okafor không phải là biết cuộc đời của bà hay đòi hỏi một tình bạn bà chưa đề nghị. Đó chỉ đơn giản là xác nhận rằng bà là người tồn tại trong không gian gần bạn — người mà sự vắng mặt trong khủng hoảng có thể được chú ý, người có thể được phản ứng khi đau khổ, và người có thể làm điều tương tự cho bạn.

Lập luận cho sự ẩn danh có sức mạnh thực sự. Trong các khu chung cư dày đặc của Hồng Kông và Tokyo, nơi hàng trăm hộ gia đình chia sẻ tường, thang máy và cầu thang, kỳ vọng kết nối xã hội có thể cảm thấy như sự áp đặt. Cuộc sống đô thị về mặt lịch sử đã hoạt động như một thỏa thuận: thành phố cung cấp sự ẩn danh để đổi lấy sự gần gũi, và nhiều người chuyển đến thành phố chính xác là vì họ không muốn những nghĩa vụ của một ngôi làng. Yêu cầu một cái tên là, theo cách hiểu đó, đe dọa thỏa thuận.

Phải công nhận, lập luận này không thể bị bác bỏ. Quyền riêng tư là lợi ích hợp lệ, và sự khó chịu khi bị tiếp cận bởi hàng xóm có giá trị, thói quen hoặc xuất thân khác với bạn là có thực. Một cái tên trao đổi tự do có thể được dùng làm phương tiện cho định kiến cũng như cho sự đoàn kết.

Dẫu vậy, sự từ chối thừa nhận hàng xóm bằng tên không phải là hành động trung lập — đó là một sự rút lui nhỏ, có chủ ý khỏi kiến trúc của sự nhận biết lẫn nhau làm cho cuộc sống cộng đồng có thể chịu đựng được. Khi các vụ cháy chung cư ở Hồng Kông những năm 2010 được nghiên cứu về hành vi của người sống sót, các nhà nghiên cứu phát hiện rằng kiến thức trước đó về tên hàng xóm tương quan với phản ứng nhanh hơn với tiếng gõ cửa và tỷ lệ hỗ trợ sơ tán cao hơn. Cái tên không phải là xa xỉ phẩm xã hội; đó là cơ chế tối thiểu của sự tin tưởng ở mức độ thấp mà phản ứng khẩn cấp phụ thuộc vào.

Điều gì bị mất khi những người hàng xóm từ chối tên của nhau không phải là sự ấm áp của cộng đồng — mà không ai nợ ai — mà là lớp thừa nhận mỏng manh phân biệt một người hàng xóm với một người lạ. Lớp đó không có trọng lượng cho đến khi nó được cần đến. Đến lúc đó, cái tên không phải là sự lịch sự; đó là sự khác biệt giữa một cánh cửa mở ra và một cánh cửa vẫn đóng.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay claim knowing a neighbour's name creates?
options: A close personal friendship with shared obligations.
The minimum social infrastructure necessary for communal living.
A legal obligation to assist the neighbour in emergencies.
A community as warm and connected as a village.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay's opening what-cleft specifies 'not intimacy but something more modest and more necessary: the minimum social infrastructure of a shared building'.
mcQuestions.hint: The essay explicitly rejects 'intimacy' — what smaller claim does it make instead?

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the 'bargain' that urban life historically offers?
options: Lower rent in exchange for community participation.
Anonymity in exchange for physical proximity to many people.
Good infrastructure in exchange for higher taxes.
Social connection in exchange for giving up garden space.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay states 'the city offers anonymity in exchange for proximity' — this is the implicit bargain many city-dwellers rely on.
mcQuestions.hint: Two things are being exchanged — find the sentence with 'in exchange for'.

mcQuestions.prompt: According to the research cited in the essay, what did prior knowledge of neighbours' names correlate with?
options: Higher rates of neighbour disputes and complaints.
Lower rates of crime in residential buildings.
Faster door responses and higher evacuation assistance rates during fires.
Increased use of neighbourhood watch programmes.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay cites research on Hong Kong apartment fires showing 'faster responses to knocks at the door and higher rates of evacuation assistance'.
mcQuestions.hint: The research is mentioned in the fourth paragraph — what two specific outcomes are listed?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'the architecture of mutual recognition' mean in the essay?
options: The physical design of apartment buildings that encourages community.
The small, ongoing social acknowledgements that allow people to co-exist in shared spaces.
The official neighbourhood watch programmes established by local government.
The cultural norms of a specific city or country.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Architecture' here is a metaphor — the essay uses it to describe the underlying structure of small acknowledgements (like knowing a name) that makes communal living possible.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Architecture' is used metaphorically — what structure is the essay describing?

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's concession regarding the right to anonymity?
options: Anonymity is only acceptable in Tokyo, not in Western cities.
Privacy is a legitimate interest, and a shared name can be used as a vehicle for prejudice.
Anonymity reduces the risk of conflict between neighbours.
City-dwellers have a legal right to remain anonymous.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay grants that 'privacy is a legitimate interest' and that a name 'can be used as a vehicle for prejudice as much as for solidarity'.
mcQuestions.hint: Re-read the paragraph beginning with 'Granted' — two separate concessions are made.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay mean by 'the refusal to acknowledge a neighbour by name is not a neutral act'?
options: It is illegal not to introduce yourself to your neighbours.
Choosing not to know a neighbour's name actively withdraws from the minimal social infrastructure of communal living.
Being rude to a neighbour has serious social consequences.
In some cultures, not knowing names is considered very offensive.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay argues refusal is 'a small, deliberate withdrawal from the architecture of mutual recognition' — not simply a passive absence of connection, but an active reduction of it.
mcQuestions.hint: The essay characterises refusal as 'deliberate withdrawal' — what is being withdrawn from?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase in the essay contrasts a social luxury with a practical necessity?
options: the thin layer of acknowledgement that separates a neighbour from a stranger
The name is not a social luxury; it is a minimal mechanism of the low-level trust on which emergency response depends
Urban life has historically functioned as a bargain
A name freely exchanged can be used as a vehicle for prejudice
mcQuestions.explanation: The semicolon in 'not a social luxury; it is a minimal mechanism' creates a sharp contrast between what the name is not (optional nicety) and what it is (practical necessity).
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the sentence that explicitly contrasts 'luxury' with 'mechanism'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'low-level trust' mean in the context of neighbours?
options: A deep, personal friendship built over many years.
The minimal assumption that a neighbour is not a threat, sufficient to open a door or respond to a call.
A legal agreement between neighbours not to disturb each other.
An app-based rating system for neighbourhood safety.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Low-level trust' refers to the minimal, baseline assumption of non-threat — not deep friendship, but enough to act on during an emergency.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Low-level' suggests minimal, not deep — what kind of trust is just enough to open a door?

mcQuestions.prompt: What is meant by 'the warmth of community — which nobody is owed'?
options: Warm temperatures in shared communal spaces.
A deep sense of belonging and connection that people have no right to demand from neighbours.
The financial benefits of community membership.
The legal protection provided by being part of a registered neighbourhood group.
mcQuestions.explanation: The parenthetical 'which nobody is owed' clarifies that the essay is NOT arguing for warmth or friendship — only for the minimal acknowledgement of a name.
mcQuestions.hint: The parenthetical phrase clarifies what the essay is NOT arguing for — something larger than its actual claim.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to opt out of community' mean?
options: To move out of a neighbourhood.
To choose not to participate in any form of social connection with those around you.
To join a different community than the one you live in.
To formally resign from a neighbourhood association.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Opt out' means to choose not to participate — here, it refers to the active decision to have no social acknowledgement with neighbours.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Opt out' means to choose not to participate — what is being opted out of here?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a passive construction to report a finding without naming the agent?
options: Urban life has historically functioned as a bargain.
A name freely exchanged can be used as a vehicle for prejudice.
prior knowledge of neighbours' names correlated with faster responses to knocks at the door
What a name shared between neighbours creates is not intimacy
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Can be used' is a passive modal that removes the agent — 'a name … can be used as a vehicle for prejudice' does not say who uses it that way.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'can be' + past participle — that is a passive modal construction.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay's final image of 'a door that opens and one that stays shut' represent?
options: The literal safety of a fire door in an apartment building.
The metaphorical difference between a neighbour who responds to a crisis and one who does not.
The cost of good-quality door locks in urban apartments.
The physical barrier between public and private space.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay closes with this image to make the abstract argument concrete — the name (or its absence) determines whether, in a crisis, a neighbour's door opens.
mcQuestions.hint: The essay's final sentence translates the abstract argument about names into a single concrete image.

mcQuestions.prompt: What is 'reciprocal knowledge' as used in the context of neighbours?
options: Knowledge shared in a school or university setting.
Information that both parties share about each other, such as each other's names.
Official records kept by building management.
Legal knowledge about shared property rights.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Reciprocal' means going both ways — 'reciprocal knowledge' means both neighbours know something about each other, including names.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Reciprocal' means two-directional — what kind of knowledge goes both ways between neighbours?

mcQuestions.prompt: What register is maintained in 'a small, deliberate withdrawal from the architecture of mutual recognition'?
options: Informal and conversational.
Technical and scientific.
Formal and analytical, appropriate for B2 argumentative writing.
Literary and poetic.
mcQuestions.explanation: The phrase uses precise, abstract vocabulary ('architecture', 'mutual recognition') and a controlled structure suitable for formal argumentative prose at B2 level.
mcQuestions.hint: Consider the vocabulary level and sentence structure — which register best describes this?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best signals a pivot from the concession back to the main argument?
options: Furthermore,
Therefore,
Even so,
In addition,
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even so' acknowledges the validity of the concession while returning to the main argument — 'despite what I just granted, here is why I still disagree'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Even so' means 'despite this being true, the following is also true'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay identify as the key difference between a neighbour and a stranger?
options: Neighbours have legal rights that strangers do not.
Neighbours know each other's daily routines.
A thin layer of acknowledgement — including knowing each other's names.
Neighbours share financial responsibilities for a building.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay states that names represent 'the thin layer of acknowledgement that separates a neighbour from a stranger' — without names, there is no meaningful distinction.
mcQuestions.hint: The essay uses the word 'separates' to describe what distinguishes neighbour from stranger.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Do you know your neighbours' names? If not, what has stopped you from introducing yourself, and do you think that barrier is a personal choice or a reflection of the kind of society you live in?
